title: Electron.js Desktop App Development
description: Learn to build cross‑platform desktop applications with Electron.js. The course walks you through installing Electron, the main‑ and renderer‑process model, IPC, security, native OS integration, data persistence, auto‑updates, packaging, code‑signing and publishing. By the end you’ll be able to ship production‑grade desktop apps using plain JavaScript (or any front‑end framework) with Electron.

lessons.title: What is Electron?
lessons.content:
# What is Electron?

Electron combines **Chromium** (the browser engine) and **Node.js** (the JavaScript runtime) into a single binary, letting you write desktop apps with web technologies.

* **Main process** – runs Node, controls windows, menus, file‑system, etc.
* **Renderer process** – a Chromium tab that displays your UI (HTML/CSS/JS).
* Communication between them happens via **IPC**.

---
#### Quick hello‑world diagram
```
┌───────────────┐      ┌───────────────────┐
│   Main (Node) │─────►│   Renderer (HTML) │
│  BrowserWindow│◄─────│   Chromium Engine │
└───────────────┘      └───────────────────┘
```
keyPoints: Electron bundles Chromium + Node.js into one runtime.
Two process types: **main** (Node) and **renderer** (Chromium).
Inter‑process communication (IPC) connects them.
Write UI with familiar web tech – HTML, CSS, JavaScript.

lessons.title: Installing Node, npm & Electron
lessons.content:
# Installing Node, npm & Electron

1️⃣ **Node & npm** – download from https://nodejs.org (LTS version). Verify:
```bash
node -v   # e.g. v20.8.0
npm -v    # e.g. 10.5.0
```
2️⃣ **Create a project folder** and initialise:
```bash
mkdir my‑app && cd my‑app
npm init -y   # creates a bare package.json
```
3️⃣ **Add Electron as a dev‑dependency**:
```bash
npm install --save-dev electron
```
4️⃣ **Add a start script** to `package.json`:
```json
"scripts": { "start": "electron ." }
```
Now `npm start` will launch Electron.
keyPoints: Node.js is required – Electron ships with its own copy of Node.
Install Electron locally (`--save-dev`) to keep the project portable.
`npm start` runs `electron .`, which looks for `main` entry in `package.json`.
All further commands (packaging, building) use the same local Electron binary.

lessons.title: Electron Architecture – Main vs Renderer
lessons.content:
# Electron Architecture – Main vs Renderer

* **Main process** – a regular Node.js environment. It creates and manages windows, handles OS‑level APIs (menus, dialogs, auto‑updates) and can run any npm package.
* **Renderer process** – a Chromium web page. Each `BrowserWindow` gets its own renderer. It cannot directly require Node modules unless you enable `nodeIntegration` (which is **not recommended** for security).
* **Preload scripts** sit between the two. They run in a tiny isolated context, expose a safe API to the renderer via `contextBridge`.

---
#### Visual overview
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Main[Main Process (Node)] -->|creates| Browser[BrowserWindow]
    Browser -->|loads| Renderer[Renderer Process (Chromium)]
    Main -->|exposes via| Preload[Preload Script]
    Renderer -->|calls| Bridge[contextBridge API]
```
keyPoints: Main process runs Node, renderer runs Chromium.
Never enable `nodeIntegration` in production – use a preload script instead.
`contextBridge` lets you expose a **whitelisted** API to the UI safely.
Each window has its own renderer; they do **not** share global variables.

lessons.title: Your First Electron App – Hello World
keyPoints: `app.whenReady()` waits for Electron to finish initialisation.
`BrowserWindow` creates a native window that loads local HTML.
`window-all-closed` quits the app on all platforms except macOS (standard behaviour).
The HTML UI is completely separate from the Node main process.

lessons.title: Project Structure & npm Scripts
lessons.content:
# Project Structure & npm Scripts

A common layout:
```
my-app/
├─ src/            # renderer source (HTML/JS/CSS or a framework)
│   ├─ index.html
│   └─ renderer.js
├─ main/           # main‑process code
│   └─ main.js
├─ preload/        # preload scripts (secure bridge)
│   └─ bridge.js
├─ package.json
└─ .gitignore
```
Add helpful scripts:
```json
"scripts": {
  "start": "electron .",
  "dev": "concurrently \"npm:watch-main\" \"npm:watch-renderer\"",
  "watch-main": "nodemon --watch main --exec electron .",
  "watch-renderer": "vite --config vite.renderer.config.js",
  "build": "electron-builder"
}
```
`concurrently` and `nodemon` are optional helpers that restart the app on changes.
keyPoints: Separate **main**, **renderer**, and **preload** code into distinct folders.
Use npm scripts to automate development (`npm run dev`).
A `watch‑renderer` script can run a bundler (Vite, Webpack) for hot‑reloading UI.
Keep `package.json` clean – expose only what the app needs.

lessons.title: Creating BrowserWindows – Options & Features
lessons.content:
# Creating BrowserWindows – Options & Features

`BrowserWindow` accepts an options object that controls size, frame, transparency, security, etc.
```js
const win = new BrowserWindow({
  width: 1024,
  height: 768,
  title: "My Electron App",
  show: false,               // create hidden, show later for smooth startup
  webPreferences: {
    preload: path.join(__dirname, '../preload/bridge.js'),
    contextIsolation: true,   // must be true for security
    nodeIntegration: false,
    enableRemoteModule: false
  }
});
```
Common useful flags:
* `transparent: true` – frameless windows.
* `alwaysOnTop: true` – stay above other apps.
* `resizable: false` – fixed‑size UI.

Show the window after it’s ready:
```js
win.once('ready‑to‑show', () => win.show());
```
keyPoints: `webPreferences` are the security gate‑keeper – always enable `contextIsolation` and disable `nodeIntegration`.
Create the window **hidden** (`show:false`) then reveal it on `ready-to-show` for a smooth experience.
Use `preload` to point at a bridge script that will be loaded before any renderer code.

lessons.title: Loading Local HTML, Remote URLs & Custom Protocols
keyPoints: `loadFile` loads a local HTML file relative to the app's directory.
`loadURL` can point to any HTTP(S) address but must be used with strict CSP.
Custom protocols (`protocol.registerFileProtocol`) let you treat bundled assets like files.

lessons.title: Menus, Application Menu & Tray Icons
keyPoints: `Menu` and `MenuItem` let you build native menus that work on macOS, Windows and Linux.
Use the `role` property for common actions (`quit`, `undo`, `copy`, …).
`Tray` creates a persistent icon in the system tray / menu bar.

lessons.title: Inter‑process Communication (IPC)
keyPoints: `ipcRenderer.invoke` → `ipcMain.handle` returns a **Promise** (clean async API).
`ipcRenderer.send` → `ipcMain.on` is fire‑and‑forget (no response).
Never expose the raw `ipcRenderer` object; use a **preload bridge** with a whitelisted API.
All messages are serialized; you can pass JSON‑compatible data only.

lessons.title: Using Node.js APIs in the Main Process
keyPoints: The main process can require any npm package, including native addons.
Use async/await with the `fs.promises` API for clean file handling.
Child processes (`exec`, `spawn`) let you run external commands.
All heavy or privileged work should stay in the main process.

lessons.title: File System Access – Reading & Writing
keyPoints: `app.getPath('userData')` gives a safe folder for persistent app data.
Use the **Promise‑based `fs` API** for async reads/writes.
Always handle errors – the file may not exist on first run.
Show a native **dialog** (`dialog.showMessageBox`) before destructive actions.

lessons.title: Preload Scripts & Secure Context Bridge
keyPoints: `contextBridge.exposeInMainWorld` creates a **whitelisted** API that the page can use.
Keep `nodeIntegration` **false** and `contextIsolation` **true** for security.
All IPC calls become **Promise‑based** (`invoke`) or simple event listeners (`on`).
Preload scripts are the only place where Node APIs can be mixed with the renderer.

lessons.title: Security Best Practices
lessons.content:
# Security Best Practices

| Recommendation | Why it matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| **Enable `contextIsolation`** | Prevents the page from accessing Node globals.
| **Disable `nodeIntegration`** | Stops arbitrary scripts from requiring Node modules.
| **Use a preload bridge** | Exposes only the API you intend.
| **Set a strong `Content‑Security‑Policy`** | Blocks XSS and unwanted remote scripts.
| **Validate all IPC data** | An attacker could craft a malicious message.
| **Enable `sandbox: true` for renderers** (optional) | Runs the renderer in a Chromium sandbox, further limiting privilege.
| **Avoid `eval`/`new Function`** | Execution of arbitrary code is a common injection vector.
| **Sign your app** (macOS & Windows) | Prevents tampering and improves user trust.

**Example CSP header in renderer HTML**
```html
<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" content="default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data:;">
```
keyPoints: `contextIsolation` and `nodeIntegration` are the two most important flags.
Never trust data coming from the renderer – always validate in the main process.
A strict CSP eliminates many XSS avenues even if an attacker injects HTML.
Code‑signing is required for macOS notarization and Windows SmartScreen.

lessons.title: Native Dialogs – Open & Save Files
keyPoints: `dialog.showOpenDialog` returns `{canceled, filePaths}`.
`dialog.showSaveDialog` returns `{canceled, filePath}` – the path the user chose.
All dialog calls must be made from the **main** process (or via IPC).
You can customize filters (`{ name: 'Images', extensions: ['png','jpg'] }`).

lessons.title: System Notifications
keyPoints: Notifications are created via the **`Notification`** class (main process).
They automatically respect the OS's Do‑Not‑Disturb settings.
You can add **action buttons** on macOS/Windows.
Remember to request permission on macOS for sending notifications.

lessons.title: Integrating Front‑End Frameworks (React/Vue)
lessons.content:
# Integrating Front‑End Frameworks (React/Vue)

Electron does not care which UI library you use – just bundle it as you would for a normal web app.

### Using Vite with React
```bash
npm create vite@latest renderer -- --template react
cd renderer
npm install
npm run build   # produces dist/
```
In **main.js** point the window at the built `index.html`:
```js
win.loadFile(path.join(__dirname, '../renderer/dist/index.html'));
```
### Using Vite with Vue
```bash
npm create vite@latest renderer -- --template vue
```
**Hot‑reload during development** – run Vite dev server and point the window to `http://localhost:5173`.
```js
win.loadURL('http://localhost:5173');
```
Remember to **disable `nodeIntegration`** and use a **preload bridge** for any privileged calls.
keyPoints: Bundle the UI with any tool (Vite, Webpack, Parcel) – output static files.
During development you can load the dev server URL for hot‑reloading.
Production builds are simply loaded with `loadFile` from the `dist` folder.
All communication between UI and OS must go through the preload bridge.

lessons.title: Bundling Renderer Code – Vite & Webpack
10 mins
keyPoints: Vite uses native ES modules for lightning‑fast hot‑reload; Webpack is more configurable for complex pipelines.
Output the bundle to a folder the Electron main process can load (`dist/`).
Configure the bundler to **exclude Electron built‑ins** (`externals` in Webpack).
Keep source maps (`devtool: 'source-map'`) for easier debugging.

lessons.title: State Management Across Processes
keyPoints: Avoid keeping duplicate copies of state in multiple renderers – use a **single source of truth** in the main process.
`ipcRenderer.invoke` is perfect for *get* operations, while `send` works for *set* without waiting for a response.
Libraries like **electron-store** simplify persistence (JSON file in userData).
Emit change events (`store:changed`) so UI updates automatically.

lessons.title: Data Persistence – electron-store, SQLite & LowDB
keyPoints: `electron-store` offers a simple key/value store with automatic JSON serialization.
SQLite provides a powerful relational DB – use `better-sqlite3` for synchronous API or `sqlite3` for async.
`lowdb` is a tiny JSON‑file DB useful for prototypes or settings.
All persistence logic should stay in the **main** process for security.

lessons.title: Auto‑Updates with electron‑updater
keyPoints: `electron-updater` works with **electron-builder** to handle all platforms.

`autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify()` checks for a new version and shows a notification.
* Listen for `update‑available`, `update‑downloaded`, `error` events.
Never expose the updater API directly to the renderer – wrap it in the preload bridge.
Code‑sign your binaries; unsigned apps will be rejected by macOS Gatekeeper.

lessons.title: Packaging & Distribution – electron‑builder
lessons.content:
# Packaging & Distribution – electron‑builder

**electron‑builder** is the most popular tool for creating installers for Windows (`nsis`), macOS (`dmg`, `pkg`) and Linux (`AppImage`, `deb`).

### Install
```bash
npm install --save-dev electron-builder
```
### Add build config to `package.json`
```json
"build": {
  "appId": "com.example.myapp",
  "productName": "MyApp",
  "directories": { "output": "dist" },
  "files": [ "dist/**/*", "main/**/*", "preload/**/*", "package.json" ],
  "mac": { "target": "dmg" },
  "win": { "target": "nsis" },
  "linux": { "target": "AppImage" }
}
```
### Scripts
```json
"scripts": {
  "build": "electron-builder",
  "dist": "npm run build"
}
```
Run `npm run build` – you’ll get platform‑specific installers in the `dist/` folder.

### Code signing (macOS)
1. Create an **Apple Developer ID** certificate.
2. Add to `build` config:
```json
"mac": {
  "identity": "Developer ID Application: Your Name (TEAMID)"
}
```
### Code signing (Windows)
* Obtain a **code‑signing certificate** (pfx file)
* Add to config:
```json
"win": {
  "certificateFile": "cert.pfx",
  "certificatePassword": "your‑password"
}
```
keyPoints: `electron-builder` can output **installers** for all major OSes from a single command.
Specify which files to include with the `files` array – include the compiled renderer bundle.
Code‑signing is required for macOS Gatekeeper and Windows SmartScreen.
After the build you can upload the installer to GitHub releases for auto‑update discovery.

lessons.title: Code Signing – macOS & Windows
lessons.content:
# Code Signing – macOS & Windows

### macOS notarization (Gatekeeper)
1. Enroll in the Apple Developer Program.
2. Export a **Developer ID Application** certificate.
3. Sign the app bundle:
```bash
codesign --deep --force --options runtime --entitlements entitlements.plist \
  --sign "Developer ID Application: Your Name (TEAMID)" MyApp.app
```
4. Notarize with `xcrun altool` (or `notarytool` in newer Xcode):
```bash
xcrun notarytool submit MyApp.app --apple-id YOUR_ID --password APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD --team-id TEAMID
```
5. Once notarized, staple the ticket:
```bash
xcrun stapler staple MyApp.app
```

### Windows code signing
1. Purchase a **code‑signing certificate** (or use a free OpenSSL self‑signed cert for testing).
2. Sign the executable with `signtool`:
```bash
signtool sign /f cert.pfx /p YOUR_PASSWORD /tr http://timestamp.digicert.com /td sha256 /fd sha256 dist/MyApp Setup.exe
```
3. Verify:
```bash
signtool verify /pa /v dist/MyApp Setup.exe
```

Both platforms require the signature to be **embedded** before creating the installer; `electron-builder` can run the signing step automatically if you provide the appropriate config.
keyPoints: macOS: sign with **Developer ID**, then notarize via Apple’s notary service.
Windows: use `signtool` (or `osslsigncode` for open‑source certs).
The signature must be applied **before** the installer is built, or the installer must sign the contained binaries.
A missing or invalid signature will cause the app to be blocked or shown as untrusted.

lessons.title: Creating Installers – NSIS (Windows), DMG (macOS), AppImage (Linux)
lessons.content:
# Creating Installers – NSIS (Windows), DMG (macOS), AppImage (Linux)

### NSIS (Windows)
`electron-builder` uses **NSIS** under the hood. You can customise the installer via the `nsis` object in `package.json`:
```json
"nsis": {
  "oneClick": false,
  "perMachine": true,
  "allowToChangeInstallationDirectory": true,
  "include": "build/installer.nsh"
}
```
A custom NSIS script (`installer.nsh`) can add extra steps (e.g., install a service).

### DMG (macOS)
`electron-builder` creates a **DMG** that displays a drag‑to‑Applications window.
You can tweak the appearance:
```json
"dmg": {
  "icon": "build/icon.icns",
  "background": "build/dmg-bg.png",
  "contents": [
    { "x": 130, "y": 220 },
    { "x": 410, "y": 220, "type": "link", "path": "/Applications" }
  ]
}
```

### AppImage (Linux)
`electron-builder` bundles everything into a **self‑extracting AppImage**.
Add Linux specific config if you need desktop icons:
```json
"linux": {
  "target": "AppImage",
  "category": "Utility",
  "icon": "build/icon.png"
}
```

Run `npm run build` and you’ll find `MyApp-Setup.exe`, `MyApp.dmg`, and `MyApp.AppImage` in the `dist/` folder.
keyPoints: NSIS creates a Windows installer (EXE) – you can customise UI and actions via an NSIS script.
DMG gives macOS users a drag‑to‑Applications installer; you can set background images and icons.
AppImage packages a portable Linux binary that runs on most distributions without root.
All three installers are generated automatically by **electron‑builder** when the appropriate targets are declared.

lessons.title: Debugging – DevTools, Remote Debugging & Logging
keyPoints: `win.webContents.openDevTools()` opens Chromium DevTools for a renderer window.
Use `--remote-debugging-port` to attach a debugger from another machine or CI.
`electron-log` gives you file‑based logging for the main process.
Listen to `render-process-gone` to detect and log renderer crashes.

lessons.title: Testing Electron Apps – Spectron & Playwright
keyPoints: Spectron is the original Electron test framework; Playwright is the recommended modern alternative.
Use **E2E tests** to verify window creation, menus, and IPC interactions.
Unit‑test the main process like any Node module (Jest/Mocha).
Run tests in CI by launching Electron with `--headless` or using Playwright’s headless mode.

lessons.title: Performance Profiling – Chrome DevTools & Electron‑Performance
lessons.content:
# Performance Profiling – Chrome DevTools & Electron‑Performance

### Chrome DevTools (renderer)
* Open DevTools → **Performance** panel.
* Record a session, look for **long tasks**, **layout thrashing**, **paint** time.
* Use **`performance.now()`** in your code to measure custom sections.

### Main‑process profiling
```js
const { performance } = require('perf_hooks');
const start = performance.now();
// … heavy work …
const duration = performance.now() - start;
console.log(`Main work took ${duration.toFixed(2)} ms`);
```
### Memory profiling
* DevTools → **Memory** → take a heap snapshot.
* Look for detached DOM trees or large JS objects.
### Use `electron‑performance` (third‑party)
```bash
npm install electron-performance
```
```js
const { monitor } = require('electron-performance');
monitor(app);
```
It logs CPU, memory and event‑loop lag.
keyPoints: DevTools’ **Performance** panel works for renderer processes out of the box.
Measure main‑process work with Node’s `perf_hooks` API.
Take heap snapshots to find memory leaks (e.g., forgotten listeners).
`electron-performance` gives you periodic CPU & memory stats in the console.

lessons.title: Accessibility – Keyboard, ARIA & High‑Contrast
lessons.content:
# Accessibility – Keyboard, ARIA & High‑Contrast

* **Keyboard navigation** – ensure every interactive element is focusable (`tabindex`, proper `<button>` tags).
* Use **ARIA landmarks** (`role="dialog"`, `aria‑label`) to describe custom components.
* Test with **NVDA** (Windows) or **VoiceOver** (macOS) to hear how the app is read.
* Electron respects the OS’s **high‑contrast** mode; style your UI with relative colors (`currentColor`, `systemColor`).
* Example of an accessible custom button in a renderer:
```html
<button id="saveBtn" aria-label="Save document">💾 Save</button>
```
* In the main process you can query the OS’s accessibility settings:
```js
const { systemPreferences } = require('electron');
const highContrast = systemPreferences.isHighContrastColorScheme();
```
keyPoints: All UI elements must be reachable via keyboard (`Tab`, `Enter`, `Space`).
Use proper semantic HTML and ARIA attributes for custom widgets.
Electron automatically follows the OS’s **high‑contrast** setting if you use system colors.
Test with screen‑readers on each platform to ensure a usable experience.

lessons.title: Capstone Project – Cross‑Platform Note‑Taking App
lessons.content:
# Capstone Project – Cross‑Platform Note‑Taking App

In this project you will combine everything you’ve learned.

## Requirements
1. **Create**, **edit**, and **delete** plain‑text notes.
2. Store notes in **electron‑store** (JSON file in the user data folder).
3. Use **IPC** to fetch and persist notes – UI never touches the file system directly.
4. Add a **system tray** icon with a menu: *Show*, *New Note*, *Quit*.
5. Implement **auto‑update** using `electron-updater` (you can point to a GitHub repository you create for the demo).
6. Package the app for **Windows (NSIS)**, **macOS (DMG)** and **Linux (AppImage)** with `electron-builder`.
7. Sign the macOS build (you can skip actual notarization for the demo, but include the steps).
8. Write **unit tests** for the main‑process store logic using **Jest** and **E2E tests** for the UI with **Playwright**.

## Suggested Folder Layout
```
my‑notes/
├─ main/          # main‑process code
│   └─ main.js
├─ preload/       # bridge script
│   └─ bridge.js
├─ renderer/       # UI (plain HTML/JS or any framework)
│   ├─ index.html
│   └─ renderer.js
├─ package.json
└─ electron-builder.yml (optional custom config)
```

When you finish, run `npm run build` and you should see three installers in `dist/`. Submit the entire project folder (source + build config + test files) as a zip.
keyPoints: All file I/O lives in the **main** process (via electron‑store).
Use a **preload bridge** to expose CRUD functions (`getNotes`, `addNote`, `deleteNote`).
Add a **system tray** for quick actions and a native **menu** for macOS.
Configure `electron‑builder` to output installers for all three major platforms.

questions.question: Which combination of settings is considered the most secure for a BrowserWindow?
What does `autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify()` do?
options: Immediately downloads and installs the update without user consent.
Checks a remote server for a new version and shows a system notification if one is available.
Opens a dialog asking the user to download the update from a website.

questions.question: In a preload script, how should you expose a function that returns a promise from the main process?

practicalTests.title: Hello World Electron App
practicalTests.description: Create a minimal Electron application that opens a window displaying "Hello, Electron!". Use a separate `main.js` and `index.html` and add a start script in `package.json`.
practicalTests.instructions:
1. `npm init -y`
2. `npm install --save-dev electron`
3. Create `main.js` (create BrowserWindow and load `index.html`).
4. Create `index.html` with a heading.
5. Add "start": "electron ." to `package.json`.
6. Run `npm start` and verify the window appears.
Submit the three files (`package.json`, `main.js`, `index.html`).

practicalTests.title: IPC – Renderer asks Main for App Version
practicalTests.description: Implement a preload bridge that lets the renderer call an async function to get the app version from the main process.
practicalTests.instructions: 1. In `main.js` add `ipcMain.handle('app:getVersion', () => app.getVersion())`. 2. Create `preload/bridge.js` that exposes `window.electronAPI.getVersion` using `contextBridge` and `ipcRenderer.invoke`. 3. In `index.html` add a `` and a script that sets its text to `await window.electronAPI.getVersion()`. 4. Ensure `nodeIntegration` is **false** and `contextIsolation` is **true**. Submit `main.js`, `preload/bridge.js`, `index.html`, and `package.json`.

practicalTests.title: File Open Dialog via IPC
practicalTests.description: Create a menu item that opens a native file‑open dialog and displays the selected file path in the renderer.
practicalTests.instructions:
1. Add a menu entry (`MenuItem` → click handler) that sends `ipcMain.handle('dialog:openFile')`. 2. In the main handler use `dialog.showOpenDialog` (allow single selection) and return the path. 3. Expose the call in the preload bridge as `window.electronAPI.openFile()`. 4. In the UI, add a button that calls `window.electronAPI.openFile()` and places the result into a `
`. Submit `main.js`, `preload/bridge.js`, `index.html`, and `package.json`.

practicalTests.title: Persist Notes with electron‑store
practicalTests.description: Implement a simple note‑taking UI that saves notes to disk using electron‑store through IPC.
practicalTests.instructions:
1. Install `electron-store`.
2. In the main process create a Store instance and expose `getNotes`, `addNote`, `deleteNote` via IPC (handle calls).
3. In the preload bridge expose async wrappers for those three actions.
4. Build a renderer UI:
   * Textarea for note content.
   * "Add" button → calls `addNote`.
   * List of saved notes each with a "Delete" button.
   * On load, fetch notes via `getNotes` and render.
5. Ensure the store file lives in the user data folder (default behavior).
Submit the complete source folder (`main.js`, `preload/bridge.js`, `renderer/*`, `package.json`).

practicalTests.title: Package the App for All Platforms
practicalTests.description: Configure electron‑builder to produce Windows (NSIS), macOS (DMG) and Linux (AppImage) installers.
practicalTests.instructions:
1. Install `electron-builder` as a dev dependency.
2. Add a `build` section to `package.json` (appId, productName, files, win, mac, linux targets).
3. Add a `sign` configuration for macOS (identity placeholder) and Windows (certificate placeholder).
4. Run `npm run build`.
5. Verify that `dist/` contains `MyApp Setup.exe`, `MyApp.dmg`, and `MyApp.AppImage`.
6. Provide a short `README.md` explaining how to run each installer.
Submit the complete project (including `package.json`, `electron-builder` config and a screenshot of the `dist` folder).
